title: Chick Brooding Cabinet
summary: A tall, legged brooding cabinet with a hardware-cloth mesh bottom, a tilt-down cleanout door, and a shelf wrapped in wire mesh for ventilation.
description:
This cabinet raises baby chicks up off the ground on 2x2 legs, with a hardware-cloth mesh bottom that lets droppings fall through instead of collecting in bedding — the same basic idea as a wire-bottom brooder cage, just built as real furniture instead of a wire pen. The sides are ripped from a single sheet of 3/4" plywood into three 15-1/2"-wide strips, referred to as "1x16" throughout this plan, which is worth remembering when you're reading the cut list.

The bottom door tilts down for easy cleanout, and the middle shelf gets hardware cloth stapled to its underside the same way the back does — ventilation matters as much as containment when you're raising chicks indoors or in a shed. Build the doors to fit your actual openings rather than to a fixed number, since a hand-built face frame is never perfectly identical opening to opening.

tools.note: Rips the plywood sheet into the 15-1/2" side strips.
Attaches the hardware cloth to the back and the underside of the middle shelf.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 3 strips 15-1/2" wide by 8 ft long
1/4" plywood or similar sheet material, for the back
Hardware cloth or chicken wire, 36" wide

cutList.note: Add more than three if your mesh needs extra support.
A fourth shelf is optional and not shown in the reference photo.
Hinged to tilt down for cleanout.

steps.title: Rip the sheet and cut the parts
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" sheet into three 15-1/2"-wide strips 8 ft long — the plan calls these '1x16' throughout — then crosscut two sides at 60", three shelves at 36" and the top at 39". Watch the yield: two of the three strips have to give a 60" side plus a 36" shelf, which is exactly 96" with nothing left for saw kerf, so cut the 60" pieces first, take the kerf out of the shelves, and then trim all three shelves to match each other. From the 2x2 cut four legs at 66" and eight front and back trim pieces at 36"; from the 1x2 cut four side trims and three grated-bottom supports at 15-1/2" and two top trims at 39"; from the 1x3 cut four door stiles at 24-3/4" and four rails at 12-3/4"; and cut the 1x8 cleanout door to 35-3/4", which uses nearly the whole 3 ft board.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 3 strips 15-1/2" wide by 8 ft long

steps.title: Prep and trim the sides
steps.body: Start with the two 60" x 15-1/2" sides. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along both long edges and across the top of each, on the inside faces so they stay hidden — every shelf, the top and all the front and back trim fastens through them, and drilling inside an assembled five-foot box is miserable work. Glue and screw a 15-1/2" 1x2 side trim across the top and the bottom of each panel, flush with the panel's edges. Lay the two finished sides face to face and confirm they match before you go any further.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 3 strips 15-1/2" wide by 8 ft long

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Set a 66" 2x2 leg against each long edge of a side panel and drive 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws through the holes you already drilled, keeping the leg flush with the panel's outside face. The legs run 6" longer than the 60" panels and the plan doesn't say where that goes — the whole point of this cabinet is standing the chicks up off the ground, so keep the leg tops flush with the panel tops and let all 6" stand below as the legs. Stand each side assembly up and check the legs are square to the panel before moving on.

steps.title: Join the sides into a box
steps.body: Stand the two side assemblies parallel with their inside faces toward each other and join them with the eight 36" 2x2 trim pieces — four across the front and four across the back, screwed into the legs. That 36" length sets the inside width, and every shelf is cut to the same 36", so measure between the side panels at the top and at the bottom and confirm both read the same before you fasten it all. Put a level across the top and compare the diagonals; a five-foot open-fronted box racks easily with only trim holding it.

steps.title: Build the mesh bottom and the cleanout door
steps.body: Run the three 15-1/2" 1x2 grated-bottom supports front to back across the bottom of the box, spaced evenly across the 36" width and screwed to the front and back trim — they carry the hardware cloth and, through it, the chicks. Add more supports if the mesh sags between them, and buy extra 1x2 to do it: the cut list's two boards are already almost fully used. Cut a piece of hardware cloth to the opening, lay it over the supports, and staple it down all round with 1/2" staples, folding the cut edges under so no wire ends point up where a chick can reach them. Then hinge the 35-3/4" 1x8 cleanout door along its bottom edge at the front so it tilts down for cleanout, and fit a latch to hold it shut.
materials: Hardware cloth or chicken wire, 36" wide

steps.title: Add the shelves
steps.body: Set the shelves in on the front and back trim and screw through the pocket holes in the sides into their ends. Each is a 36" x 15-1/2" panel — the full inside width — so it should drop straight in; if one binds, the case is racked rather than the shelf mis-cut. The plan gives no shelf heights, so set them where you want them, marking both sides from the same reference so each shelf goes in level. Leave the middle shelf's underside clear of anything that would stop hardware cloth lying flat against it later.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 3 strips 15-1/2" wide by 8 ft long

steps.title: Add the top
steps.body: Set the 39" x 15-1/2" top on and screw up into it through the pocket holes along the top edges of the sides. It runs 39" over a carcass measuring 37-1/2" across, so it overhangs 3/4" at each side — set that overhang equal at both ends before you drive more than the first screw. Then glue and pin the two 39" 1x2 top trims along the front and back edges to cover the plywood edge.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into 3 strips 15-1/2" wide by 8 ft long

steps.title: Add the back
steps.body: Lay the cabinet face down and fit the 1/4" back panel, lining it up on one side and the top and then pushing the case until it meets the other two edges before fastening the rest. Trim it to your actual case first — the cut list gives it as 38-1/2" wide against a carcass that measures 37-1/2" across the sides. Glue and fasten right around the perimeter and into the back edge of each shelf; on a tall box that is open at the front, the back panel is the main thing keeping it from racking.
materials: 1/4" plywood or similar sheet material, for the back

steps.title: Build the doors and add the mesh
steps.body: Each door is two 24-3/4" 1x3 stiles with two 12-3/4" rails between them, which makes a 17-3/4"-wide door — two side by side just fill the 36" opening. Measure your actual opening and cut the rails to suit before you assemble, since a hand-built face is never identical opening to opening, and join the corners with pocket holes and glue on a flat surface so the doors don't wind. Hang each on a set of hinges and add the knobs, handles and latches. Last, staple hardware cloth over the back opening and to the underside of the middle shelf with 1/2" staples — check your material first, because 4 ft of 36"-wide cloth will not cover the floor, the shelf and the back, and ventilation matters as much here as containment.
materials: Hardware cloth or chicken wire, 36" wide

images.alt: Legged chick brooding cabinet with mesh bottom and tilt-down cleanout door
